title: Harden chart_utils: length validation, TypedDict, and separate_label bounds check
analysis: chart_utils.py currently has a single function group_top_n() that takes a dict with 'labels' and 'values' and groups entries beyond N into an 'Other' bucket. The {labels, values} dict shape is an implicit interface used across write_journal.py (get_operations_breakdown, get_agent_breakdown), performance.py (get_loop_histograms), chart_utils.py (group_top_n), app.py (memory_graphs_partial), and Jinja templates (memory_graphs.html). No TypedDict exists anywhere in the dashboard project. Existing test_chart_utils.py has 8 passing tests in a TestGroupTopN class covering passthrough, grouping, empty input, and sort-order preservation. All tests use pre-sorted, matching-length data — no edge-case coverage for mismatched lengths or unsorted input. separate_label() does not exist in the codebase and must be created as a new function. Python 3.11+ is the target (from __future__ annotations already used).

steps.description: Write test that ChartData TypedDict can be imported from chart_utils and has 'labels' and 'values' keys with correct types (list[str] and list[int | float]). Use typing.get_type_hints to verify annotation structure.
Define ChartData as a TypedDict in chart_utils.py with 'labels': list[str] and 'values': list[int | float]. Export it in the module's public API.
Write test that group_top_n raises ValueError when len(labels) != len(values) — test both labels-longer and values-longer cases. Also test that matching-length input still works (regression guard).

Add length validation at the top of group_top_n: raise ValueError('labels and values must have the same length') when len(labels) != len(values). Update function signature to accept ChartData and return ChartData.

Write test that group_top_n sorts unsorted input descending by values before grouping. Test cases: (1) unsorted input with len > n produces correct top-N + Other, (2) unsorted input with len <= n returns values in descending order, (3) already-sorted input is unchanged (regression against existing tests).

Add internal descending sort by values at the start of group_top_n (after length validation): zip labels+values, sort by value descending, unzip back. This replaces the 'sorted by convention' assumption.

Write test for separate_label basic behavior: given data {'labels': ['a', 'b', 'c'], 'values': [30, 20, 10]} and label='b', returns (remaining ChartData without 'b', extracted value 20). Verify original data is not mutated.
Create separate_label(data: ChartData, label: str) -> tuple[ChartData, int | float] that finds label by name, removes it from both lists, returns (remaining_data, extracted_value).
Write test for separate_label when target label not found: returns a copy of the data and 0 as the extracted value. Verify it does not raise.
Handle the label-not-found case in separate_label: if label not in data['labels'], return (shallow copy of data, 0).

Write test for separate_label bounds check: when values is shorter than labels and the target label's index is beyond values length (e.g., labels=['a','b','c'], values=[10], label='c' at idx 2 but values has only 1 element), raises ValueError with descriptive message.

Add bounds check in separate_label: after finding idx, verify idx < len(values) and raise ValueError if not. Message: "label '{label}' at index {idx} has no corresponding value (values length: {len(values)})".

Write test for separate_label with mismatched-length input where the label IS within bounds of values. E.g., labels=['a','b','c'], values=[30, 20], label='a' (idx=0, within bounds) — should succeed and return remaining data and value 30. This validates bounds check doesn't over-reject.

Verify step-13 test passes with current implementation (no code change expected — validates bounds check precision). If it fails, adjust bounds check to only reject when idx >= len(values), not when overall lengths mismatch.

design_decisions.decision: Define ChartData TypedDict in chart_utils.py rather than a separate types.py module
design_decisions.rationale: chart_utils.py is the canonical chart data manipulation module and the only file that operates generically on this shape. Avoiding a new file minimizes churn. Other modules (write_journal.py, performance.py) can import from here if they adopt the type later.

design_decisions.decision: group_top_n sorts internally rather than asserting sorted input
design_decisions.rationale: Defensive sorting (zip, sort descending by value, unzip) is safer than assertion because: (1) assertions can be stripped with python -O, (2) the function's contract says 'sorted descending by convention' which is fragile, (3) sorting pre-sorted data is O(n) in Python's Timsort so there's no performance penalty for correct callers.

design_decisions.decision: Length validation raises ValueError, not AssertionError
design_decisions.rationale: ValueError is appropriate for invalid argument values and cannot be disabled with -O. It also follows the convention in dashboard.data.utils.parse_utc which raises TypeError/ValueError for bad input.

design_decisions.decision: separate_label returns (data_copy, 0) when label not found, rather than raising
design_decisions.rationale: Not finding a label is a normal condition (e.g., chart data with no 'Other' entry). Raising would force try/except at every call site. Returning 0 as the default value is a safe no-op for summation/display contexts.

design_decisions.decision: separate_label raises ValueError when idx >= len(values) rather than returning a default
design_decisions.rationale: If a label exists but has no corresponding value, the input data is structurally corrupt. Silently returning a default would mask data corruption. ValueError forces callers to fix the root cause.

design_decisions.decision: ChartData uses list[int | float] for values, not Sequence or Number
design_decisions.rationale: All producers (write_journal.py queries, performance.py histograms) return plain lists of int. Using list[] matches runtime types exactly and keeps the TypedDict simple. Union with float covers potential future use (percentages, averages).

reuse.how: Add new test classes (TestChartDataType, TestGroupTopNValidation, TestSeparateLabel) following the same pattern: class-based grouping, descriptive docstrings, one assertion per behavior.
Already present in chart_utils.py — TypedDict annotations will be string-evaluated at runtime, compatible with this import.
Keep the core slicing logic intact — insert validation and sorting before the existing code path. Existing tests serve as regression guards.
